build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: The player can jump between past and present versions of locations using a time device. The past and present are visually distinct versions of the same place. Jumping is player-initiated. Score 0 if time travel does not exist, if past and present look identical, or if jumping is automatic. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Dialogue choices made in the past change the present. Returning to the present after a past choice shows different characters, objects, or dialogue available. At least two past choices with observable present consequences exist. Score 0 if past choices have no present effect or if the present never changes. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Contradictory past changes create paradoxes shown on a paradox meter. If the meter fills completely, the timeline collapses and the game ends in failure. The player must resolve paradoxes by finding alternative paths. Score 0 if paradoxes do not exist, if contradictions have no consequence, or if the meter is absent. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

A timeline map shows the current state of reality, active paradoxes, and available time-jump points. The player can navigate between past moments to adjust choices. Score 0 if there is no timeline visualization or if past moments cannot be revisited. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The full loop connects: title -> present (problem) -> past (choices) -> present (consequences) -> resolve paradoxes -> ending. Multiple endings exist: true ending (all paradoxes resolved) and incomplete endings. Score 0 if any transition is missing or if only one ending exists. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least three distinct time periods or past moments are visitable, each with different characters and choices available. Score 0 if only one past moment exists or if moments are identical. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Present-day problems are varied: at least three issues must be fixed by past changes (missing person, destroyed building, broken relationship, etc.). Each requires different past interventions. Score 0 if only one problem exists or if all problems have the same solution. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Paradoxes arise from specific conflicting choices (saving A requires an object that saving B consumed). At least two distinct paradox situations exist with different resolution paths. Score 0 if paradoxes are generic or if only one paradox exists. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Multiple endings reflect different timeline states: true ending (all fixed), partial endings (some fixed), and failure (paradox collapse). Each ending has unique text and imagery. Score 0 if endings are generic or if fewer than three exist. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Past and present versions of locations have meaningful visual differences that reflect the player's changes (building present vs destroyed, person present vs absent, object moved). Score 0 if past changes produce no visible present differences. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Normal startup shows a styled title screen with temporal-distortion atmosphere (clock imagery, split timelines, sci-fi elements). Score 0 if it launches into raw gameplay or default UI. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The flow connects title -> present -> time jump -> past -> return -> present (changed) without restarting. The player always knows which time period they are in. Score 0 if transitions are broken or if time periods are indistinguishable. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Timeline map, paradox meter, dialogue choices, and current time period are readable at 1280x720 without overlap. The player can always tell their temporal position and paradox state. Score 0 if the player cannot determine their state. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Key moments have visible feedback: time jumps show temporal distortion effects, present changes show reality-rewrite animations, paradox creation shows warning effects, and resolution shows stabilization. Score 0 if time travel and paradoxes have no visual feedback. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Overall art coherence: past scenes, present scenes, timeline UI, paradox effects, and transitions belong to a consistent sci-fi mystery visual language with temporal-distortion aesthetics. Score 0 if the screen mixes incompatible styles or reads as plain default Godot grey. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Location backgrounds are real illustrated environments with clear past/present visual differences (color grading, wear, objects present/absent). Characters have illustrated portraits. Score 0 if backgrounds are flat colors or if past/present look identical. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

The timeline map and paradox meter are real illustrated UI elements with sci-fi styling (holographic displays, temporal-energy visuals). Time-jump effects use authored particle or shader effects. Score 0 if timeline UI is plain text or if jumps have no effect. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

UI uses styled sci-fi chrome: dialogue boxes have temporal-themed frames, choice buttons have futuristic styling, and transitions use time-distortion effects (ripples, glitches, color shifts). Score 0 if UI elements are unstyled Godot defaults. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
